Kernel.pause() pauses :) preserving all state.
Kernel.continue() resumes from where it left off.

It is for embedding the kernel into another application.
Parent application will provide pause, start, stop logic.
Coroutines may also decide to pause the kernel. 
(Of course they can't restart again.)

This enables to perform all operations within the thread of main
application. Communication between curio and parent app
will still be via queues, events, etc... Coroutines in curio can even
call sync functions of the parent app.

A more general question.  Would it make sense for `defer_cancellation()` to behave more like the ``timeout_after()`` function?  For example:

    result = await defer_cancellation(coro(args))

Or as a context manager::

    async with defer_cancellation():
           statements
           ...

Just thinking out loud...

There is a listener for new rooms and joining them here: https://github.com/skoczen/will/blob/4233c20ce69d04fa1310459d56a67d13e1c69711/will/listener.py#L66

When a new room is created it checks to see if the room is in the list of allowed rooms to join. However if you don't have rooms listed, because you want the bot in all rooms, then that list of allowed rooms is set at boot as a list of all rooms.

file bar.md:

``````{.markdown}
---

panflute-path: /home/reenberg/src/pantable/pantable
---

```{.table}
1,2,3
```
``````

However running `pandoc -F panflute -w markdown /tmp/bar.md` generates the AttributeError.

If I just run the pantable filter, then i can see that there is actually a difference in the table that is producered.  Running `pandoc -F pantable -w markdown /tmp/bar.md`:

```{.markdown}
  --- --- ---
  1   2   3
  --- --- ---


```

However `pandoc -F panflute -w markdown /tmp/bar.md` with an updated panflute-filters results in:

```{.markdown}
  ------------------------ ------------------------ ------------------------
  1                        2                        3
  ------------------------ ------------------------ ------------------------


```
Which can be converted fine by only running the panflute2csv filter in both of the mentioned ways.

Thus I can only conclude that panflute must be doing something special (wrong?) when running multiple filters automatically

So by round-trip invariance you mean that parsing a GFF file and printing out its features should yield the original GFF file. I.e `encode(decode(value)) == value` + parsing/writing the file.

If `encode = urllib.parse.unquote` and `decode = lambda x: x.replace(',', '%2c')` (and more `replace`/regex or something equivalent for all the other characters that must be encoded), the above property holds, unless I'm missing something. Since `%` must be encoded, `unquote` will not decode too much. Further, since each character either must be encoded or musn't be encoded (according to GFF spec, i.e. there is no choice in the matter), and there is only one way to encode each character, there is no ambiguity in re-encoding characters.

I would find it most intuitive if the values are stored decoded in the database and are only re-encoded in the return of `str(feature)` as the latter tries to show the line as it appeared in the file.

I don't think that the default should be to delete the non-hashed files.

Putting aside the backwards-compatibility issue, deleting the files is an inherently riskier option. If you keep the files when you don't need them you've simply wasted a bit of space. If you delete the files when you do need them, your site will break. Deleting the files is a performance optimization that you should do when you're sure that 100% of your static file references will be dynamically generated by Django. The *default* option should be to do the thing that just works, regardless.

In formulas in Excel if a sheetname contains special characters or looks like a date or a number it needs to be enclosed in quotes. Like this:

    ='2105_04_03'!A1

So, your program should work with the following change:

```python
main.write('A1', "='{}'!A1".format(other_name))
```
This works with Excel 2007 and 2011.

    =$2105_04_03.A1

I don't know if that is the standard syntax in LibreOffice.

I also tried with a similar file created in Excel and that gave the same result.

Perhaps you can test in Excel as well.

For those that also run Django tests on their tasks that use QueueOnce, here's how I mock the redis backend.

*test_something.py*
```python
....
from django.test import TestCase
from fakeredis import FakeStrictRedis
from mock import patch

...

class AsyncTaskTest(TestCase):

    def setUp(self):
        self.get_redis = patch('celery_once.backends.redis.get_redis')
        self.mocked_redis = self.get_redis.start()

        self.redis = FakeStrictRedis()
        self.mocked_redis.return_value = self.redis

    @patch('yourapp.tasks.call_command')
    def test_your_task(self, mockCall):
        """
        Assert that your_task
        calls your_command only once.
        """
        your_task.delay()

        # Set redis key with TTL 100 seconds from now
        # so subsequent tasks won't run
        self.redis.set('qo_your_app.tasks.your_task', int(time()) + 100)

        your_task.delay()
        your_task.delay()

        mockCall.assert_called_with('your_command')
        self.assertEqual(mockCall.call_count, 1)

    def tearDown(self):
        self.get_redis.stop()
```

> I have created a "backup" functionality using the Django Reversion library
> for an object that keeps revision of a registered model, including the
> related objects (foreign keys etc.).
>
> Let's use the following case as an example.
>
> class Newspaper:
>    title = models.CharField()
>
> class Article:
>    newspaper = models.ForeignKey('Newspaper')
>
> When the user clicks to keep a backup of a newspaper object, a revision of
> the newspaper is created along with the already created articles, under the
> same revision. I did it so that when the user chooses to revert back to the
> latest backup, all related objects under the same revision to be reverted.
>
> The problem starts when a new article of the newspaper is created after
> the revision is created. The issue is that if the user chooses to revert to
> the previous revision (the one before creating the new article), the new
> article will still be there because it was not registered in the latest
> revision.
>
> Furthermore, I don't want to create a new revision every time a new
> article is created because then there might be other changes included that
> the user will not want to include to the revision.
>
> What I think might be a suitable solution, is when a new article is
> created, to create a Version of the article and register it in the latest
> created revision of the newspaper, but I don't know if it's possible using
> the functions provided by the API (maybe using add_to_revision?)

And yes, it’s a fundamental change to how admin rollback is achieved. Before, it was a load of little hacks to smash the revision data into the admin form. Now, it uses a database savepoint to temporarily restore the DB to a point in time, render the page, then roll back the save point.

It’s a bit of an abuse of save points, but it seems to work in all DB engines. There are some caveats, such as it breaking MyISAM tables in MySQL (but that’s no longer the default table format in recent versions), and breaking for a transaction isolation of read uncommitted (which isn’t the default isolation level in any database anywhere). The benefit is that a huge number of fragile hacks are gone, and that a load of small things that didn’t work now work. :)

I managed to find the solution below. As the eBay API reference documentation points out to [retrieve specific site information, use [DetailName elements](http://developer.ebay.com/devzone/xml/docs/reference/ebay/GeteBayDetails.html).

Since I wasn't using a `DetailName` element with `GeteBayDetails`  to narrow down the api call my guess is the amount of data retrieved too big for my python editor and was making it crash. 

A list of [DetailNameCodes](http://developer.ebay.com/devzone/xml/docs/reference/ebay/types/DetailNameCodeType.html) can be found here. 

I've also added an xml parser to prettify the output so it comes out in a nested list instead of one long string which makes the output much easier to read. Hope this helps Thanks to @timotheus for your contribution too. 

My code below:

```
from ebaysdk.trading import Connection as Trading
from ebaysdk.exception import ConnectionError
import xmltodict
import json

siteid = '3'

def dump(api, full=False):

    if api.warnings():
        print("Warnings" + api.warnings())

    if api.response.content:
        print("Call Success: %s in length" % len(api.response.content))

    print("Response code: %s" % api.response_code())
    print("Response ETREE: %s" % api.response.dom())

    if full:
        print(api.response.content)
        print(api.response.json())
        print("Response Reply: %s" % api.response.reply)
    else:
        dictstr = "%s" % api.response.dict()
        print("Response dictionary: %s..." % dictstr[:150])
        replystr = "%s" % api.response.reply
        print("Response Reply: %s" % replystr[:150])

try:       
    api = Trading(domain='api.ebay.com', config_file='ebay.yaml', siteid=siteid, debug=True)
    
    details = {"DetailName" : "SiteDetails"}
    response = api.execute('GeteBayDetails', details )
    dump(api)
    
    ### decode the xml string
    xml_string = response.content.decode("utf-8")
    
    ### convert the xml string to dictionary
    xml_dict = (xmltodict.parse(xml_string))

    ### format in a nice nested list
    print(json.dumps(xml_dict, indent=2))

except ConnectionError as e:
    print(e)
    print(e.response.dict())

```

I think it is cleaner and more intuitive to have a specific setting for providing an extra filter that it is AND'ed to the final filter. That's why I called that setting LDAP_AUTH_SEARCH_EXTRA_FILTER in my pull request. It's optionally given for finer filtering of users not only based on the user mapping settings, but also based on extra, application-specific criteria (group membership, etc.).

I understand that it may seem there is some inconsistency between syntaxes of settings that will be used for final filtering of users. However, if we look at the settings from a user's perspective, we may see that we actually need settings for 2 different purposes. First, we need settings for user and user attribute mapping with the simpler way to express that being it how it is done now: a pythonic mapping. Second, we need some setting for extra, application-specific filtering and the simpler way to express that would be in the LDAP filter syntax. I think that it's quite clear that users will be looked up at LDAP based on the mapping settings and, if needed, filtered further down based on an extra filtering setting.

Regarding *pre-releases* (alpha/beta/rc): When building towards such release, it still makes sense to have developmental builds in between, instead of incrementing the alpha release (`a1`, `a2`, ...) for each commit. With the code you merged, we can do:

    $ git tag -a 1.2a1.dev
    $ # do commits and get versions 1.2a1.dev0, 1.2a1.dev1, 1.2a1.dev2, etc.
    $ tag tag -a 1.2.a1  # finally release alpha1
    $ git tag -a 1.2a2.dev  # start building towards alpha2

The same for beta and rc releases.

I think this makes more sense and is suggested/accepted in PEP.

Maybe for post-releases, what you suggest might make sense. But the PEP still allow developmental builds of post release:

> X.Y.postN.devM   # Developmental release of a post-release

But it also states:

> The use of post-releases to publish maintenance releases containing actual bug fixes is strongly discouraged. In general, it is better to use a longer release number and increment the final component for each maintenance release.

So, I wonder if it makes sense to support a convention which is discouraged.

It does solve a different use case. linalg.gemm deals with a batch of gemm operations. The extension in this PR relaxes the constraint to have only the leading dimension used as a batch coordinate (by allowing that the coordinate associated with the matrix rows is at a different axis).
 
Batching and tensordot are two different concepts. That is why Theano also has a batched_tensordot() operator. While a single matrix-matrix product can be formulated either as gemm or as a tensordot, a batch of gemms can not be formulated as a single tensordot. Suppose we have a batch-gemm on shapes A= (I, M, K) and B = (I, K, N) where the first coordinate is the batch coordinate, then batch_gemm will return a shape (I, M, N) while tensordot(A, B, [[2], [1]]) will return a shape (I, I, M, N), i.e. it is a completely different computation.   
So this PR allows more flexibility about which axis are batch coordinates, but it is not anything that brings in any tensordot-functionalities.

Calling `initialize` multiple times in one application doesn't have defined behavior, and I wouldn't necessarily expect it to succeed without some between-run cleanup (in this case calling `.clear_instance()` on the subapp is what's needed).

One change that probably does make sense, and should fix both cases: subapp uses `.instance` *unconditionally*, when it should probably be used on the condition that the parent is the current global instance. So only if the parent is the instance should it clear and reassign as is done now. This would actually fix both the above cases exactly as they are because global instances are not in use. It wouldn't fix the hypothetical issue of calling `.initialize()` to launch subcommands multiple times on the registered global instance, but I don't think that should be supported. This is implemented in #487.

In addition to my comments in #1801, I think we need to introduce some mechanism in addition to `Parameter.parse()`, something like `Parameter.parse_from_command_line`. The idea is that it has a default implementation to just call out to `parse`. Meanwhile `parse` may only perform pure logic `prase_from_command_line` could do all kinds of conveniences for the user, in this case, it could convert a relative path to an absolute path.

Technically, this [call to parse](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/cmdline_parser.py#L133) should be call `parse_from_command_line` instead.

As this issue is still affecting me in pip, I've continued to investigate. I was able to replicate the error simply by installing a namespace package, having that namespace package also minimally present on sys.path (in `.`) and to import pkg_resources, e.g.:

```
pip install jaraco.itertools
mkdir jaraco
echo '__import__("pkg_resources").declare_namespace(__name__)' > jaraco/__init__.py
python -c "import pkg_resources"
```

Note that the `jaraco` directory need not have the `declare_namespace`
When initializing the master working set, pkg_resources activates the `jaraco.itertools` distribution, which causes the `declare_namespace('jaraco')`, which fails on the `_handle_ns('jaraco', '')`.

The issue only occurs when the installed namespace package was built with Setuptools 31.0.0 or later (with the newest `-nspkg.pth` file technique). I don't encounter the issue if installing an older wheel that doesn't have that newer technique.

@f2prateek In previous versions, there was a timeout of 0.5s for reading items from the queue. So messages are uploaded when:

- queue limit is hit, or
- there's a gap of more than 0.5s waiting for the next message to be enqueue.

So as long as the queue is not empty, the maximum time between messages a user has to wait for upload is 0.5s (worst case scenario: messages are pushed into the queue at 0.49s interval).

The new default value provides not exactly the same behaviour, but that's as close as we get given the meaning of the parameter is different (upload interval vs. queue reading timeout).

That said, I'll remove that sentence from the commit message to avoid confusion.

Hi Vighnesh,

You may want to take a look at the Cython implementation of labeling in `ndimage` [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/master/scipy/ndimage/src/_ni_label.pyx). It uses `PyArrayIterObject` from Cython, and even has some examples of fused data type specialization to copy cast data from the iteration point into a contiguous buffer. A lot of it is just a convoluted mess of function pointers, that would be infinitely more clearly written in C directly, but I digress...

Anyway, @njsmith pointed you in the wrong direction, but with good intentions. ;-) `PyArrayIterObject` is indeed exposed as a Python object, only it is not `np.nditer`, but `np.flatiter`. As Nathaniel pointed out, you can call `PyArray_ITER_NEXT` on the return of `np.flatiter`. There are some issues with the way yu are accessing the data in the array, but I'll comment on that inline.

As you mentioned elsewhere, this is highly overlapping with @aman-iitj and his GSoC project. This said, I by no means want to push you away from working on this, but rather pull you in so that your work is coordinated with Aman's.

I think it would also make more sense to use a `rho_resolution` parameter that represents the width of a bin in the rho direction, rather than a `num_rho` parameter (as I've currently done), which represents the number of bins to use.

We need a decision on the names for the border modes to move this forward.  In summary, what was being called 'reflect' in the current codebase, matches the behavior of mode 'mirror' of ``ndimage`` (but does match 'reflect' of ``np.pad``).  No equivalent of ``ndimage`` 'reflect' (or ``np.pad`` 'symmetric') was previously provided.

In the current PR, I have made 'mirror' and 'reflect' match the ``ndimage`` behaviour.  I think this approach is the simplest since a number of other routines in ``skimage`` already take ``ndimage`` mode arguments.  However, it was suggested to perhaps use the border mode names from ``np.pad`` (and ``skimage.util.pad``) instead.  There is no clear consensus on this from the core devs who responded.  Do any of the other core devs want to chime in?  @stefanv maybe?

If we go with the ``np.pad`` names there would be 3 names that would require a name conversions whenever routines from ``ndimage`` are called :
- 'edge' -> 'nearest'
- 'reflect' -> 'mirror'
- 'symmetric' -> 'reflect'

np.pad modes below:
![numpy_pad_modes](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6528957/9073519/08907d5c-3ad0-11e5-9774-855bcfb3bb04.png)

ndimage modes (with bug in wrap mode):
![ndimage_modes](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6528957/8581306/da1f2ace-258d-11e5-898f-e35f1c0b0f8e.png)

OpenCV has slightly different names as well:
![opencv_border_modes](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6528957/9073640/b79ead78-3ad0-11e5-8c36-8361f01ddb56.png)

@atlithorn Here is a working example. 

It happens when you want one resource to handle multiple routes. Note that if you try to `curl -X POST localhost:5002/HelloWorld/ASDF` you will get an error but  `curl -X POST localhost:5002/HelloWorld` will succeed

```
from flask import Flask, jsonify
from flask_restful import Resource, Api
from flask_swagger import swagger

app = Flask(__name__)
api = Api(app)

class HelloWorld(Resource):
    def post(self):
        """
        Create a new user
        ---
        tags:
          - users
        definitions:
          - schema:
              id: Group
              properties:
                name:
                 type: string
                 description: the group's name
        parameters:
          - in: body
            name: body
            schema:
              id: User
              required:
                - email
                - name
              properties:
                email:
                  type: string
                  description: email for user
                name:
                  type: string
                  description: name for user
                address:
                  description: address for user
                  schema:
                    id: Address
                    properties:
                      street:
                        type: string
                      state:
                        type: string
                      country:
                        type: string
                      postalcode:
                        type: string
                groups:
                  type: array
                  description: list of groups
                  items:
                    $ref: "#/definitions/Group"
        responses:
          201:
            description: User created
        """
        return {'hello': 'world'}
    def get(self, session_id : str) -> "http response":
        return session_id

@app.route("/spec")
def spec():
    return jsonify(swagger(app))

api.add_resource(HelloWorld, '/HelloWorld', '/HelloWorld/<session_id>')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(debug=True, port = 5002)
```

I think I found the origin the issue. 

Basically, ISO compliant datetime expressions, such as `2016-11-29T14:22:36+00:00`, when exposed via raw URL query param, the datetime value must be URL safe and encoded accordingly first. Most HTTP clients do this transparently, but others do not.

The above datetime must be encoded as `2016-11-29T14:22:36%2B00:00` in order to turn sensitive characters URL safe. That works as expected and can be parsed accordingly by connexion/Swagger parser.

I'd like to share with you my method for splitting a terminal inside a guake tab with byobu.
My goal is to reuse the guake custom command feature to split the terminal with byobu shortcuts.

- Install byobu
- Install xdotool
- Use my own guake version at https://github.com/Ozzyboshi/guake ( I will explain later why)
- Create a json file like this:

[test.txt](https://github.com/Guake/guake/files/187424/test.txt)

- rename test.txt in test.json and select it from guake prefs->general->custom command file path

Now go to your terminal and start 'byobu'
Now to horizontal split just right click on the terminal and go to 'custom commands' ->  Byobu vertical split

There are some other commands in the test file, the one i use more, you can customize it according to your needs and your byobu shortcut configuration.

The test file is quite straightforward, it just tells guake to emulate some keypresses with xdotool, please notice the syscall section, if this field is present and set to 1, guake will execute a system call instead of sending the command to the terminal, this is very useful because users don't want to see the xdotool command on the screen and because it works under remote sessions like telnet or ssh.
The syscall field is implemented in my custom guake version, if @Stibbons likes it i will be happy to issue a pull request.
I know this is not what was requested but please tell me if you think this is a viable workaround.

You know, as we are going to put this to logstash (so, probably, we've got distributed logs case here), it can be hard to reproduce an issue. Sometimes, you don't even have an access to the production server.

so, I'd rather vote for a more extendable way of handling exceptions.
I mean, at the moment is a plain https://github.com/exoscale/python-logstash-formatter/blob/master/logstash_formatter/__init__.py#L91 format_exception.
But there's https://pypi.python.org/pypi/tblib, or Flask (or Werkzeug, I'm not really in context there) also serializes a traceback somehow to put it on html debug page.

So, I'd suggest to move traceback formatting function to the class atrribute so that it's easy for anyone to override it. like this:

```
class MyFancyTracebackFormatter(LogstashFormatter):
    tb_formatter = my_fancy_formatter
```

Dear Arthur,
Thank you for trying out memote and reporting on your difficulties here. I'll try to address them point-by-point.

> memote runs, but the stdout and stderr output is difficult to understand and the index.html file generated is empty

Our default branch on GitHub is "develop" which you installed. We are currently changing the way reports are generated from being jinja2 template based to an Angular app. Since we're also changing around the structure quite a bit, I have disabled the HTML reports until the Angular app is in. You should be able to see the old version of the HTML report if you installed memote 0.4.6 directly from PyPi (`pip install memote==0.4.6`).

Although reading messages on the command line is never fun, I'm saddened to hear that you found the messages difficult to understand because we actually but some effort into making them clearer and more informative. Do you have any recommendation for how they could be improved?

So the slight dilemma is that 0.4.6 has the old HTML report which makes the overview better but the develop branch has the "improved" messages but no report. We are aiming for a next memote release with the Angular report by the end of next week. May I ask you to try again with the next release (probably 0.5.0) when it's out?

> 'memote -h' and 'memote report -h' could produce more comprehensive documentation. E.g., many lines are truncated

The truncation is a "feature" of click. You can see the full text from the command line if you ask for the help on the final sub command, e.g., `memote report snapshot -h`. I agree that the documentation could use a lot of TLC. We will focus on documentation when we have stabilized the major moving parts of memote.

I don't believe Andy's example unless the dummy target `is_jvm`.  This behavior has existed from day 1 of pants when pants kicked the ant backend - so this is ~2011 vintage behavior.  We've discussed this at Twitter numerous times - where we is me and various tweeps on devprod and otherwise, as well as in several pants summits.  The CodeGen baseclass that ProtobufGen and ApacheThriftGen inherit from determines languages to generate code from from inbound target edges in the active graph.  In other words, if I compile a set of targets where one is python and it depends on the thrift, another is java and it depends on the same thrift, ApacheThriftGen will generate python and java code.  If the active target graph has no-one depending on the thrift nothing is generated.  Its this behavior you are witnessing.  Today you must supply the langs to generate for on the CLI if you try to act on thrift targets with the apache compiler directly.  Everyone agrees this behavior is confusing to folks and needs to go.  No one so far has stepped up to fix ... over the span of 2+ years of complaints!

Generally, I like this. I think SAM can bring additional value by way of the operation story for building an application. I do have some concerns here are how does this impact big templates and having Alarms (possibly other operations things like dashboards) in the same template therefore same stack.

## Impact on Big templates

SAM uses one stack to deploy resources, so for customers who are already near the 200 CloudFormation Limit this solution doesn't work. It could be problematic for customers who are nearing the limit already and then add CloudWatch Alarms.

With that said, I do think it makes sense for the initial schema but I think the design needs to expand a little to make sure we don't break larger templates. This leads into my next concern :)

## Alarms in the same stack

I am for separation of stacks and strongly believe Alarms and other operational resources belong in their own stacks. This is mainly for `blast radius` reduction and to keep service deployments separate from updates to Alarms, Dashboards, etc. SAM only supports one stack and I think that is an actually blocker for this feature, obviously up for debate as I am just stating my opinion. From my own experiences, having the ability to have Operational Resources in their own stack was super helpful. I was able to update CW Alarms or Dashboards without having to worry about affecting our resources. This was invaluable and produced safe deployments for the team.

## Some other thoughts on the design directly:

* I see you do not have all the required properties for `AWS::CloudWatch::Alarm`. Will SAM be generating theses? How will SAM support 'ComparisonOperator' or 'Namespace'?

* How will SAM support other `AWS::CloudWatch::Alarm` Properties? Useful ones that come to mind are Dimensions, TreatMissingData, Unit, etc. Are customers of this feature going to just loose this flexibility?

I'm not sure which decorators you are referring to ``@staticmethod`` and ``@classmethod`` serve a particular purpose, but most likely what you have noticed are ``@property`` decorators. No, we have no preference for using decorators especially much, we use them when they serve a purpose. The ``@property`` for instance, is useful if you have a method, that essentially acts like a property (i.e. essentially just for reading out a value of something) but where the value needs to be calculated. I think probably the best example of the use of ``@property`` is the fictional ``Circle`` class, where you store the radius internally, but implement the diameter as a property. So, to put it shortly, no, we just use them when it makes sense (the cases above or any other time you need to wrap a functions in a start and end). Also, one should be careful about not overusing decorators, because they 'hide' code from plain sight and therefore can make it more difficult to read.

> It's not clear to me where this requirement comes from. E.g. is it something technical in our versioning code, a PEP-440 convention, or general release process convention?

If the `VERSION` tuple is a _final_ version, then the rest of the logic is switched off. This is necessary, to avoid needlessly (and dangerously!) creating `git` subprocesses to generate the version string.

Therefore, if you for instance bump to `(0, 10, 1, 'final')`, then everything build on `release-v0.10.x` will be `0.10.1` and this is a mis-labeling of all subsequent releases leading up to the first pre-release of a `0.10.2`.

> always either final or beta

So... that's kind of where I was wondering to what extent this may be more reasonable an external extension of `saspy` rather than in core, because indeed the syntax I created is very different; but I believe the syntax _needs_ to be different like this in order to gain any benefits from `saspy` over simply writing straight SAS code blocks, so that's where the dilemma will be. 

Here's what I see as the main difference in approach to the more straightforward example above; probably sounding pedantic here but it helps to lay out my reasons for desiring a more systematic tool on top of TABULATE's purely convention-driven quirks:

In your example, the actual axis statements (top, left) are simply string arguments. Without a layer of abstraction on top accepting logical conjunctions (via operator overloading, basically) into query strings in a buildable way, any script or reporting tool that uses this library will end up with very messy code, essentially concatenating many different strings manually for different conditions, instead of expressing logical conjunctions.

Eg, with string arguments, a reporting program capable of building differently shaped tables would quickly run into a lot of this kind of conditional work:

```python
top_query = 'origin '
used_classes = ['origin']
used_vars = ['enginesize']

# let's say show_horsepower_groups, show_cylinders & show_counts are options

if show_horsepower_groups==True:
  new_grouping = 'engine_type'
  used_classes.append("engine_type") # have to manually track all vars, classes used

  if (show_cylinders):
     new_grouping = new_grouping + ' | cylinders' # string concats
     used_classes.append('cylinders')

  top_query =  "%s | %s  * (horsepower * (%s))" \
     % (top_query, new_grouping,  show_counts and "mean | n" or "mean") # buggy string interpols. 
  used_vars.append("horsepower") 

cars.tabulate(classes=used_classes, vars=used_vars, top=top_query, ... #etc
```

...which gets unmanageable quickly when attempting to build more sophisticated shapes on the fly, or sharing code between functions in a more general reporting tool that might create many different table variations for differing audiences, add or remove levels at will, etc. The above, on my current syntax, would be logical expressions instead of string manipulations, to more cleanly combine many different options as needed:

```python
top_query = by_origin

if show_horsepower_groups==True:
   new_grouping = show_cylinders and (engine_type | cylinders) or (engine_type)
   stats = show_counts and (mean | n) or (mean)  
   top_query = top_query | (new_grouping * stats)
   # all logical expressions above, which build an AST-like tree that will serialize into TAB syntax later

# also no need here to maintain lists of vars, classes used; built on the fly when you call table()

cars.tabulate.table(top=top_query, left=left_query)  #etc
```
The way I see it, TABULATE's syntax is akin using a highly idiosyncratic flavor of SQL, where building statements as strings on the fly is necessarily error prone in nature (even or especially if using the Macro language, which is basically nothing more than string interpolation all the way down); libraries like SQLAlchemy or PyDAL exist largely for this reason, to prevent anyone from falling into the terrible coding practice of constructing SQL statements by appending strings to strings at length, and in order to offer a logical syntax for expressing queries as statements, which then serializes into correct syntax when ready to execute.

So, I don't know -- I appreciate the look at it. I wonder if there's either: 
  (1) a compromise syntax that would be composable / logical, yet closer to the function invocation you want; or
  (2) any merit to simply establishing a standardized way for external libraries like this to "extend" saspy as a tool.

I believe the problem here is simply an issue with openssl versions, and the apparent workaround of `ndg-httpsclient` just works if you happen to have built your `cryptography` wheel against the (old) system PyOpenSSL.  You can verify this by looking into your virtualenv like so:

`otool -L $VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cryptography/_Cryptography_cffi_* | grep libssl`

If this shows you a `/usr/local/lib/` then you are linked against homebrew; `/usr/lib` and you're linked against the system.

I was able to get Python entirely out of the loop, like so:

```
$ echo | /usr/bin/openssl s_client -verify 9 -cert "${DOCKER_CERT_PATH}/cert.pem" -key "${DOCKER_CERT_PATH}/key.pem" -CAfile "$DOCKER_CERT_PATH/ca.pem" -connect "${DOCKER_HOST#tcp://}" -servername boot2docker 2>&1 | grep Verify
    Verify return code: 0 (ok)
$ echo | /usr/local/bin/openssl s_client -verify 9 -cert "${DOCKER_CERT_PATH}/cert.pem" -key "${DOCKER_CERT_PATH}/key.pem" -CAfile "$DOCKER_CERT_PATH/ca.pem" -connect "${DOCKER_HOST#tcp://}" -servername boot2docker 2>&1 | grep Verify
    Verify return code: 21 (unable to verify the first certificate)
```

Even after years of practice, I still find OpenSSL's error messages completely inscrutable, so this needs more investigation, but it looks like the problem is something broken about how boot2docker is issuing its own certificates.  I *think* the issue might be that the server isn't setting the proper extended key usage for TLS web server authentication, but since the docker command line (and therefore the go TLS implementation) is happy with this certificate, I don't know what's different.

I have a better understanding now of what's causing Travis to fail, so let me report some of my findings.

## Repo Link

First, in the spirit of reproducibility for my reproducibility results, I created a repo with a Dockerfile that makes testing trackpy in an environment identical to Travis trivial and extremely fast (although the container takes about 2 cups of coffee to build): https://github.com/charlesreid1/trackpy-travis-docker/

Using this, I was able to build trackpy at several points in its development (I built it with several tags 0.3.1-0.3.3 and several commits,listed [here](https://github.com/charlesreid1/trackpy-travis-docker/tree/master/shared)) with an Anaconda environment identical to Travis tests, and  reproduced the data contained in the gold standards from each of these.

## Conclusions from tests

My conclusions are written up [in that repository](https://github.com/charlesreid1/trackpy-travis-docker/tree/master/shared) along with all the outputs I used to draw those conclusions, but I'll summarize here:
* Python 2.7 (both up-to-date and legacy test environments) pass the assertion tests and were identical to the gold standards to ~12 decimal places. The gold standards and the standards reproduced by the tags and commits differ by about 70 lines (only the last lines) but have 1,810 matching lines, which means they're really close and thus pass the assertion tests. The results between tags mismatched on those same last 70 lines.
* Python 3.5 results were qualitatively different (values were off by 0.1-1). However, when I sorted the reproduced standards data (and gold standards data) by x and y value, and dumped it out without an index to compare, I found that the x and y values still matched up for many of the rows, but that features were not being tracked across as many frames (so that a feature that had 8 rows in the original gold standard had only 6 in the standard reproduced with Python 3.5).
* The tags don't fail the assertion tests for number of features found, but the individual commits tested *do* fail these assertion tests. The Python 3.5 results between tags and commits largely overlapped (many identical rows), but between the tags and commits tested, there were often missing features (i.e., a feature found by tag 0.3.1 of trackpy would not be found by commit f693a2f3 of trackpy, or vice versa). This meant that the number of features between the tags and commits was similar, but deviated too much for each of the commits.

## Next steps

I still haven't figured out what's causing the O(0.1-1) deviations between Python 2 and Python 3, so I'll look into that and see if it might actually be because of Numpy or Pandas.

## Resolution

The resolution to the failing Travis problem depends on the outcome of the "next steps." Separate versions of the gold standard for Python 2 and Python 3 is kludgey, but would fix the problem as it stands.

I know this is a lot of information and a bit confusing, but have a look at [the conclusions I wrote up](https://github.com/charlesreid1/trackpy-travis-docker/tree/master/shared) and take a look in the `shared/` folder (visualizing the differences using `vim -d` is illuminating). And of course, please ask questions if you have them. I'll follow up when I have findings about the Python 2/Python 3 calculation differences.

@msarahan There were actually multiple issues the original PyYAML code is not willing to indent a scalar in a block sequence (which you only notice if you have non-scalars as well, and set `indent>2`). The scalar was always indented 2 resulting 
in `res = yaml.dump({"x": [{"b": 1}, 2]}, default_flow_style=False, indent=4)` giving

```
x:
-   b: 1
- 2
```

In [ruamel.yaml 0.11.3](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/ruamel.yaml#indentation-of-block-sequences) this is fixed and in addition there is the `block_seq_indent` keyword parameter with which you can push the dashes in a block_sequence inward. Thus 
`res = ruamel.yaml.dump({"x": [{"b": 1}, 2]}, default_flow_style=False, indent=4, block_seq_indent=2)`
gives you:

```
x:
  - b: 1
  - 2
```

I hope this gives you fine enough control. 

The authoritative source is on bitbucket, please file bugs there (including non-working example code). But in principle you should be able to install from PyPI, unless you want to dive into the gory details.

One small detail I neglected to mention: we are bundling an ld executable on Linux with python.  This was necessary to make the fallback work.  Without it, old linkers would fail, we think due to the additional LTO optimization.  We set up python's compiler flags to look in $PREFIX/bin, where we put ld, so that we're using a new enough ld to avoid issues.

The difference is between two double dashes instead of single long one.

Imagine this scenario:

```console
$ conda create -n scrapper -c tswicegood lxml
$ source activate scrapper
# do my work in my environment with lxml because I hate my life and am not using one of the other libraries on top of lxml that's nice... :-)
# realize I need to put together a django site for my scrapper
$ conda create -n web django
```

At this point if I added `tswicegood` to my channels for the `lxml` package and I had released a dev build of Django 1.9 in my main channel I just accidentally installed it into my `web` environment.  For me managing my own channels that's one thing, but what about every random channel maintainer for packages I installed?

In the context of the `scrapper` environment above, I fully expect `tswicegood` to be checked when upgrading `lxml`.  I don't expect that to effect anything in the `web` environment.  Modifying the user's Conda config does that, however.

What you describe in that example is more what I would call *backward incompatibility*, which I would say is fair game for--and actually the reason for--major version bumps.  That is, a conda environment created with a newer version of conda may crash an older version of conda when that old version tries to operate on it.  Conda environments created with older versions of conda should *absolutely* be able to be operated on with newer versions of conda.  Always.

Taking a step back from what your current implementation and conda usage... If I were designing a system for multiple conda users on the same machine from scratch, I'd probably only have a single conda executable on the whole system.  Maybe just a basic miniconda environment at `/opt/conda`, and then symlink `/opt/conda/bin/conda` to `/usr/local/bin/conda`.  That environment is obviously read-only for non-privileged users, but still executable.  In this case, there's really no "default user environment" right now, and that's actually something we might need to fix.  Conda is available as a command, but `conda install` without creating/activating an environment first will fail with a permissions error.  In this setup, the default user-writable package cache is at `~/.conda/pkgs_dirs` and the default user-writable envs directory is at ` ~/.conda/envs`.  Of course those can both be configured to other places.

Speaking of configuration, conda has fairly advanced capabilities here.  You'll probably be interested to read the blog post at https://www.anaconda.com/blog/developer-blog/conda-configuration-engine-power-users/.

The basic idea is to have a script which compiles the Vivado standard libraries like `unisim` as well as the project specific IP libraries *outside* of VUnit and then add those as external libraries to VUnit. This is how I have worked with Vivado in two different projects and taught some other people to do as well. It allows you to just check in the `.xci` files or the `.tcl` files which generates the IP:s into version control and then ask Vivado to generate the simulation models and their compile order. This external compile is done outside of VUnit and thus it uses commands hardcoded to a certain simulator which is modelsim in this case. Typically Vivado IP:s contain encrypted files which VUnit cannot scan for dependencies and GHDL cannot compile. 

It has been a while since I created this example, maybe I can update it to support more simulators.

The reason why I came up with this solution was because I saw both features in the bigger context, both of them tracking the lifecycle each and every request goes through. I don't see that both things should be logically separated, as they belong to the same thing - the request lifecycle, which possibly was not as obvious when tracing was first implemented.

Some of the decisions I made are in deed opinionated and make bigger steps without being fully discussed - that's exactly the reason why I created this working draft, to be able to discuss every change I saw necessary. I thought working on actual code that already does the thing might have helped to find the best solution faster. Fiddling timeouts in between the already comparatively long and complex code and hoping for somebody to clean everything up at some later point did not seem like a good idea to me. This solution also looked a lot cleaner and nicer to me, especially if you see how much code it actually _removed_ from the [ClientSession](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/pull/2842/files#diff-7dd84b5ef8d5eea2de1dfc5329411dfcR254).

If you don't like the discussion about tracing were having here, that is in my opinion something we can actually defer without building technical debt. The only discussion we had here related to tracing was how the async callback methods should be called. I though it would be clearly better to call them in parallel than in sequence (using `asyncio.gather` or a more fitting function even better suiting our needs). As I didn't touch the tracing.py module at all, we could just as well stay with the old sequential `for trace: await trace.send` approach (this time only implemented one in lifecycle.py) and defer all further discussion about tracing to after this PR.

If you want to go back to the drawing board, feel free to do so or propose another way. Unfortunately, I can't see the clean solution that you are proposing without making to much mess of the code.

I don't think setting sys_prefix and user both to True will do the right thing, because only one is triggered in the end, unless something changed. I thought this would through a mutually-exclusive flag error.

I think you'll need to handle `all` specially, and I see two options:

1. Follow pip's example, and remove the first one you see in the priority order.
2. go through them all in order.

Option 1. seems like the more logical behavior, to me. It has the potential disadvantage (like pip) that you have to re-run it until it shows nothing installed to be sure that it's truly gone if you have something enabled/installed at multiple levels. But it has the advantage that it only does one action.

Option 2. might better address the specific use case @ellisonbg brings up in #2149, where the real goal is "scrub every trace of this and start from scratch". However, 'disable' is not quite the right action for that use case, because `disable` sets an explicit `False` flag (thus overriding lower-priority config), whereas scrubbing every trace should pop values from config, not set them to False. That makes "remove every last trace of this" a somewhat distinct action from disable.

This ambiguity doesn't affect `uninstall`, though.

It seems like what would be really nice is if uninstalling an extension also removed enabling configuration, but that's getting pretty complicated.

It's not a poor separation of concerns until recently - the point of prospector was to be a super easy "run one command" static analysis tool so it made sense to include pylint-django because automatically tuning checks for whatever framework you were using was the initial focus.

I realise now that the propagation of requirements was another reason I didn't want to include django as a requirement of pyling-django. By removing pylint-django as a default install for prospector, it undermines the whole point which was "install, point it at code, get results with as little effort as possible".

I still maintain that not including Django as a dependency of pylint-django is a good idea, people who don't set up their test environment properly need to deal with that and including as many warnings and clues as possible in pylint-django is the best idea - hence the DjangoNotInstalledChecker. Make it easy to diagnose but don't try to second guess every environment it will be installed into because that's too many permutations.

> These attacks are very theoretical and far from really affecting current connections. Especially using AES in hardware (AES-NI), whatever its performance may be, should save you from the timing attacks. And as far as I understand it, the other attacks released by Bruce Schneier in 2009 don’t apply at all to regular TLS connections and make very specific assumptions about nonstandard round length, known plaintext and related-key knowledge.

> However, I don’t want to go into a debate about the presence or absence of AES256 security qualities. What I wanted say is that it is useless to enforce server-side cipher order (SSLHonorCipherOrder On) and list the AES256 ciphers behind their AES128 counterparts if all browsers which implement AES256 also implement AES128. So only those clients who tweak the ClientHello disallowing AES128 and only offering AES256 would actually ‘benefit’ from supporting this said-to-be weaker encryption based on AES256. Then, why have it on the list at all?

> So, presuming “SSLHonorCipherOrden On”, if someone still feels safe with these discovered AES256 weaknesses he should actually put the AES256 ciphers in front of the respective AES128 ciphers – otherwise they’re never used at all. On the other hand, if he doesn’t trust AES256 for some reason, he should just throw these ciphers off the list instead of just moving them to lower priority.

> Point is: Don’t use magical pattern matching in the SSLCipherSuite entry. Keep the list explicit and as short as possible. Understand (and possibly document) each used cipher’s strengths and weaknesses and which cipher is used by which browsers and what you think would be an appropriate time to drop it. Use SSL Labs tests early, use them often. Keep an eye on security mailing lists, and check your settings whenever you do an upgrade of any of your SSL/TLS related components (e.g., Apache/nginx, OpenSSL/LibreSSL, CPU with/without AES-NI, certificate renewal, key family upgrade RSA/ECDSA).

OK, I think I understand now. 

You are trying to use Tinode as if it's a REST API. It is **NOT** a REST API. It's a streaming publish-subscribe API. The endpoint `/v0/channels` is only used to open a websocket. It is NOT for `http post`ing packets to it. It's used only to open a websocket. Once the websocket is created, the client uses websocket API to send packets. See here on websockets: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WebSocket

Here is Go's stock implementation of websockets: https://golang.org/x/net/websocket
Here is another one that Tinode uses: https://github.com/gorilla/websocket

The API documentation is already quite clear on this point. Please do read the documentation with open mind.

As far as I undestand it (now), the main question is:
Shall we share jupyter-notebook's javascript environnement ?

### If yes
* we feel coherent with other projects (like bokeh, mpld3)
* we have the opportunity to interact with the notebook (use widgets...)
* we can fix and keep `initialize_notebook`

But
* we have to make sure not to break anything in jupyter each time we add some javascript.
* we have to make sure that nothing breaks in folium each time jupyter changes it's javascript.
* every dependency that is added into folium will need to be reported into `initialize_notebook`(or whatever it's name).


### If no
* we have a single template for generating html
* we have no risk of breaking something between folium and jupyter-notebook
* we shall go on an `iframe` that sets a separate javascript interpreter.

But
* interactions with jupyter widgets or other libraries (bokeh...) may be more difficult
* folium loads a full html page each time it displays a map


I must confess I am quite afraid about interaction issues (see an illustration on https://github.com/BibMartin/folium/blob/master/examples/javascript%20interference%20example.ipynb) and debugging javascript makes me pain. So my personal answer would be no. But I've tryied to be fair in this *pros and cons* exercise ; please tell me otherwise.

>  I think the best way to handle this would be to add a conditional rule that installs into /Library/... when DESTDIR is not set.

I was actually thinking of doing it slightly differently. I was thinking of providing an extra method(s) in ``MakeRuleCmd`` class that allows things to be installed outside the prefix and only allow it be used on OSX when the word ``python`` appears somewhere in the path. My reasoning here is that ``DESTDIR`` must always be respected. Doing this doesn't prevent a direct install on OSX because ``DESTDIR`` is not normally set so running ``make install`` would install into ``/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/...``. If ``DESTDIR`` was set then it would end up installing to ``$(DESTDIR)/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/...``.

Although branching on the value of ``DESTDIR`` inside the Makefile is possible I don't think that's a good route to go down.

'end of the world' is a bit strong indeed, but it is a bit of a wart imo. We should avoid forcing users to use things in unintuitive ways, which I think this is an instance of.

I think `partial_fit` a great idea, a lot better than overloading `fit` to do both and needing a hack to make it work.

> I've trained a siamese net with nolearn, it's working pretty well. I've even been using your trick of having an input that's double the size as the labels, and it was straight-forward.

Fair enough, I just want to make sure we don't end up forcing our users to shoehorn their use case into it -- it should be natural to extend whatever we provide to support these more exotic configurations (within reason of course, we probably can't support / think of everything).

I think the `BatchIterator` stuff might already go a bit too far. What benefits does this abstraction offer over just working with Python generators? Every new class or concept adds cognitive overhead, so for anything that goes into the main library I'd like to keep this to an absolute minimum.

> I don't know about these use cases; you'd have to enlighten me.

I was just trying to think of use cases where you have additional data streams that are more label-like, or more input-like, or something in between, to demonstrate that this dichotomy between input and labels isn't always as clear-cut as it is for many regression/classification problems. Just to show that hardcoding this dichotomy everywhere probably isn't the right way to go. Because it is so common, we can of course provide tools to make this case easier, but it should also be easy to go beyond this.

> The nolearn.lasagne docs are a big void right now. :-( There's only a couple of tutorials so far. Need to fix...

Maybe we should strive to merge it (or some derivative of it) into Lasagne proper, and fix the docs in the process?

AFAIK Theano and PyTorch are deterministic in their runs (from what ops I
have used at least) given enough seeds are set and so forth. At very least,
I have been able to get repeatable runs in both down to some low eps (1E-6
or more) tolerance. There are ways to make TF behave as well (I believe
reduce_sum is non-deterministic, maybe others), but it is a bit more
involved.

I will report back here when I get a chance to run some code.

As with your previous PR, the feature is fairly useful, but I'm not sure about the syntax. There are several issues:
- This could in theory break certain searches with the `|` character (`[` and `]` characters in #552). I don't think this is likely to be a major issue in practice, but it is worth considering.
- The syntax is not obvious, but I don't know if there is a way to make it so.
- It does not really leave too much room for other features of the sort, at least not in a consistent way.

A different syntax for these features like `[duration=long, after=2016-10-17]/query` or `/query --duration=long --after=2016-10-17` (I think I prefer the latter) would partially address these issues, but is not so concise. It could support a shorter form though, like `/query -d long -a 2016-10-17`.

> I think 80 characters are too limiting. We should increase the line limit to 120, at least. There are already existing lines that exceed the 80char limit and most people are programming on widescreen monitors, not eighty character punchcards.

Fair point. An 80 character line limit is also useful for a two column layout. So you can have a text editor in one half of the screen and test the program in the other half. It's also a fairly standard rule; PEP8 recommends a 79 character limit: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#maximum-line-length

Hi, 

There is no significant software processing steps like you mentioned. 
The pipeline for generating depth is considerably longer then usual the one on a web-cam. It still should not reach the values you'r reporting. 
150ms at 30 FPS means 5 frames are being queued somewhere:
* the camera itself does not have the memory to hold them
* the uvc-driver and the relevant framework (Media Foundation on Windows and V4L on Linux) do maintain internal frame-queues. On a fast PC with low CPU consumption, these should be empty at most times. What is the configuration that you are using? Can you give it a try on a different setup.
* librealsense does minimal image conversions on the frames. If you are running on Intel architecture, this code is well optimized and tested to take less then 2 ms. 
* librealsense also maintains an internal frame-queue when you are working with wait_for_frames API. This function is trying to synchronize between color and depth streams, causing extra latency (usually less then 15 ms) On the development branch we provide frame callbacks that let you be the first one to know about a new frame. You can give that a try.

Could you please specify what exact camera model you are using (as displayed in the device manager)?
Also, what kind of streaming do you enable? (color, depth, color+depth) At what resolution and FPS?

Hello @AleBasso80 ,
The root cause is the improper enumeration of the device as USB2 type. D400 devices require USB3 connection to satisfy both the power and bandwidth requirements. 
When the device is enumerated as USB2 the firmware switches to a "reduced capability" mode which includes only a subset of depth resolutions and formats (only low-res) and no RGB sensor.

The main reason for the improper enumeration is the HW setup: USB ports/connectors/cables/hubs.
You need to verify the HW chain step by step to fix it.

Some recent reports on this topic: [1181](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/issues/1181),  [1061](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/issues/1061),  [1148](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/issues/1148),  [902](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/issues/902)

Hi @Sandy4321,

The data that we used to train this model is from common crawl, and thus, very noisy. For this reason, there are "words" such as `qqq` that are present in our training data, and have a corresponding representation. We did not filter the words in any way, but kept the 2M most frequent "words" from the training data.

Best,
Edouard.

Hi @munichong and @saroufimc1,

It seems that you are using a third party python wrapper for fastText. The answer to this question depends on the corresponding implementation of this wrapper. In particular, I believe that in Gensim, `model[str]` will consider `str` to be a word (and not a character ngram). In some situation, this means that you cannot retrieve vectors corresponding to subwords like that.

Using the python wrapper from our project, you can obtain a list of subwords (and corresponding indices) using the method `get_subwords`. Then, using the indices, the subword vectors can be obtained by calling the method `get_input_vector`.

Best,
Edouard

@njgheorghita 

I have strong opinions about mocks and I'll try to distill them here briefly.

## Mocks are to be avoided.

- Mock based tests **tend** to end up testing the implementation and not the actual functionality.  
- It is **very** easy to have mock-based tests pass but the actual code fail.
- can end up being *leaky* if not done correctly (leaving objects patched after test cleanup, or other weird side effects)

## Signal that your code is incorrectly structured

In many cases, finding that you need to mock something is a *signal* that you need to re-factor your code to make things more modular so you can test a specific component without needing to mock out other components.

## When all else fails

Mocks **can** be ok when all other options are exhausted, or it is the pragmatic solution, or some other reason to ignore the bad things.

## But maybe don't mock things

My opinion is that if you are going to mock the request/response cycle, why not just use a dummy backend and stub out the responses as fixtures.  Unless you **need** to specifically test the HTTP backends (which should be tested in isolation), then if you're testing other functionality, then mocking out the request/response cycle seems like un-necessary overhead where a generic fixture based dummy backend would be simpler.

According to Jeff *"The final coordinate pair in the elliptical arc command must have whitespace preceding it."*. I'm unsure about that, though...

According to SVG 1.1 spec the [elliptical arc command](https://www.w3.org/TR/SVG/paths.html#PathDataEllipticalArcCommands) has the following parameters:
`(rx ry x-axis-rotation large-arc-flag sweep-flag x y)+`

The [grammar](https://www.w3.org/TR/SVG/paths.html#PathDataBNF) for it reads
```
elliptical-arc-argument-sequence:
    elliptical-arc-argument
    | elliptical-arc-argument comma-wsp? elliptical-arc-argument-sequence
elliptical-arc-argument:
    nonnegative-number comma-wsp? nonnegative-number comma-wsp? 
        number comma-wsp flag comma-wsp? flag comma-wsp? coordinate-pair
```

So if I'm not blind to the problem there is only one required `comma-wsp` which is in front of the "large-arc-flag". However (since this flag can only have the values 0 or 1) Scour would always put a space there anyway.

Ah, that is probably since the multi-gpu SGD operates in two phases:
First, data is loaded into GPU memory (in the `load_data` call).
Then, gradients are computed over slices of the in-memory dataset.

This allows us to avoid excess CPU->GPU transfers when executing multiple iterations of sgd. However, it means that data is only fed once, during load. It might work to just move the control deps to the data loading ops (`batch.initializer` in multi_gpu_impl:214). Those assign ops should definitely be run with the right placeholders set.

So with #181 we've pretty much settled on our general architecture for handling worker threads: a lower-level unbounded "thread cache" (similar to [the JDK's "cached thread pool"](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/util/concurrent/Executors.html#newCachedThreadPool--)), plus an extensible policy layer on top that runs in the trio thread.

So the remaining issue is: currently our "thread cache"'s replacement policy is "always", i.e., we don't actually have a cache, we just start a new thread every time. Maybe it would be worthwhile to re-use threads. This is a non-trivial increase in complexity, and it's primarily an optimization, so maybe it should wait until we have some real programs whose behavior we can measure.

If/when we do this, we'll need to figure out the API for interacting with the cache. At the least, we'll need a `trio.hazmat` API for grabbing a cached thread and running some code on it. There are also some configuration options you can imagine setting on a thread cache, like how long an idle thread should wait before exiting, or the thread stack size. (Not that Python lets us usefully control the latter right now.) Should we make these configurable by users? Should we allow for the creation of multiple thread cache objects with different settings? If so, then how does the user specify which one they want? (For `run_in_worker_thread` it'd just be a matter of passing in the desired thread cache, but what about `getaddrinfo`?)

We'll also want to re-use this "thread cache" for other miscellaneous threads that can't quite use the standard `run_in_worker_thread` machinery, like calling `WaitForSingleObjectEx` or `waitpid` – see #233 #4. Possibly also for some kinds of I/O on windows if we decide to use `CancelSynchronousIo` – see https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/174#issuecomment-310262200. The distinction features of these cases are: we want an unbounded admission policy, and we need special handling for cancellation. For `WaitForSingleObjectEx` and `CancelSynchronousIo` there's a special API for cancelling work, and for `waitpid` we'll want to spawn a thread at the same time we spawn the process and let it run in the background without being tied to a specific trio task.

Furthermore velocyto is not thought to work on laptops, because of the intrinsic memory usage requirements for some functions makes it unpractical to run them with just 16GB of RAM. So we don't plan to add extensive support for OS for this right now. 

However, if you still want to be able to install on Mac despite this there might be a solution.
Notice that in the github repository we distribute a precompiled `.so` extension for Mac/Darwin architecture ``speedboosted.cpython-36m-darwin.so``. So, in principle, this can be installed from source skipping the compilation step, doing the following:

Clone the repo
`git clone https://github.com/velocyto-team/velocyto.py.git`

modify the file `velocyto.py/setup.py` in the following way:

```
C_COMPILE = False
USE_CYTHON = False
```

install from source:
 - Make sure you unsinstalled previous versions `pip uninstall velocyto`
 - Move in the folder`cd velocyto.py`
 - install by `pip install .` (notice the trailing dot)
